speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. I'm interviewing Mr. Mojsze Ganz. You were on the train approaching--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: We cross-- We cross the border. The old Russian-- Russian-Polish border. At each station or village, station and village, people from the station or from the village was coming. They took off people from the train. They took off 10 people, 20 people, 30 people, 10, as many people that can have arranged work, and as many people that can arrange for accommodation according to size for the town. And we was on the train another 10 or twelve days. As we come a few stations from Vitebsk, a few stations from Vitebsk, that was the middle of the night. Two man was-- the train stopped. I don't know what was, a station, or a town, or a village. The train stopped middle of the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two men was coming with two lanterns. You know, lanterns? They took off 120 people. And they start to walk, and walk, and walk. They looked around, no houses, no people, nothing, step, pushed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long we was walking. Might be one hour, half hour, or two hours, or what I, I, I don't know, because we didn't have watches. As we're walking, from far away, I saw light. Not electric light, lamp, kerosene lamp light. We come there. Barracks. Full of barracks. They give us a, a, a hot, a wash, a place to, to have a wash. They give us a meal. They took us to the barracks, and the barracks was [NON-ENGLISH] In Russian, [NON-ENGLISH]. Not a bed, a [NON-ENGLISH]. From, from, from pieces of iron, pieces of, of wire, like beds to, to sleep. In each bed, was a big bed with straw, and a little back for the cushion, and a blanket. That was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next morning, you go up in the morning, you look around. You can't see no houses. You can't see nothing. Where we are? Where we are?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't speak nobody, because nobody was there, only two men, which I was looking for the, for the barracks, and I used to cooking for us. They can't speak Polish, they can't speak Yiddish, and I, and we can't speak Russian. But we already knew a little bit, a few words of Russian. We find out, not far from here, a few kilometers from here is a village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next, next day, they took us to work. What work we doing? They was building a road for Moskva, Minsk. And the, the road have to go through the forest. Our work was to cut the trees off from the forest, prepare it for the road. Can you imagine that we people, we, we never use to work like that. We were-- Sunday, not working day, we got off. We find that not far from here, a few kilometers from here is a little village. We went over there. We met a few Yiddish families in this village.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have time to be long, because we all had to [INAUDIBLE] there and we had to go back. Take a while till  we come back. As we come back, they, they tell us, next Sunday, you come earlier. We never talk and speak, we might be friends and something like, [? Yiddish ?] people. Next Sunday, we come more early. We-- after breakfast, we went straight there. We come there. Was 11 o'clock, something like that. They took us in their home. They make a nice meal. They started talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: They start asking, how you live in Poland. Before the war, how you live? We was asking how they was living under the Communist regime. They were very, very friendly. They would make, make up a nice meal for us, prepare it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see one man is going out from the, from, from their, from their home. Their house. Half an hour later, he comes back. He, he, he got a-- a collection outside for the-- the people. And they give it to us, a few ruble, a few zloty, they give it to us. And [? already ?] 2 o'clock, 3 o'clock, we have to go back, because take a while till we come back there. One man stood up and he's like that. Kill them. Get out from here, like that. You'll get lost here. It's not your work. Work in the forest, cut trees. You will try this. You [? will try it ?]. You [? go to the town ?]. You will be people-- like people, not like, like that. You will get lost. Kill the-- I warn you. Get out from here. Get out. I say, if a Russian man say to me like that, must be something wrong. Must be something good. When we come home, next day, two days later, we already, a few kolkhoz is already empty. More people, they're already gone. I tell the [INAUDIBLE] we have to go. We have to go. I took our little bundle [NON-ENGLISH] what we have. The little trousers, and dresses, everything we have. Went to the station. We come to Vitebsk. Big town, Vitebsk. I went straight to the government office, straight to the, to the city council, or something like that. I knew already what people was coming from. They give it to me, a paper, a recommendation to get work, and to get-- get place to live. I've got, still, the paper here somewhere. I got it, still, the paper. I would like it if you will be-- take this photo from this little paper. Very interesting for me-- and I start to work. I was working in a factory. 15,000 people was work in the factory, [INAUDIBLE] factory. 15,000 people. And my Leja, she start to work in a little [INAUDIBLE]. I was such a good worker-- the factory was building flats-- they put me on after a few months, they put me on the list to the capital to get a, get a flat. I was a good worker. And we start to build from nothing. We see anything for sale in the shops, we bought it. Even if you don't need it, something for you, but in the Russia, bought something like that. If you don't, if you don't need it, you buy it. Because if you buy something like that, it's glass. You don't need it, the glass, but you keep it. When you're needing something else, you go at the bazaar or the market, Bob, I've got something which I wanted, and you wanted the glass, we changed it, or I [? pay ?] you something. That would-- We have a big basket full of goods prepared to get the flat and to shift it in at the flat. [INAUDIBLE] we hear France are under occupation from Hitler without shot-- any shot. Romania, Bulgaria, Yugoslavia, all them, already German got up. My Leja said to me, Mojsze, look like the war will not be-- will not finish so fast. Let us got married. All right. We got married. I went with my Leja to the register office. We pay two, two rubles. We got married already, like register office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day, in the next day, my Leja told me, Mojsze, that's not a marriage. I don't like it. I don't want to get married in this way. The war, he said, will, will not last forever. We will go back sometimes to our parents, and the parents will ask us, did you got married according to Jewish law? If not, your children will be like bastards [NON-ENGLISH] According to Jewish law, if you have children without marriage, they are like bastards, you see? They are treated like bastards. She was right. But how can you find enough people to get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is religion. I-- I [INAUDIBLE]. I already got a few friends, Yiddish people, what I was working together with me in the factory. And I was asking him something like-- not-- but I was hoping something, but-- I told him, go away. Drop itl And I started to frighten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe the police will find out that I'm-- I'm talking about the religion. That mean I've-- I've propaganda religion, and I'll get in trouble. And I'll get arrested. What we can do? Again, it come in my mind like my grandmother was to say. God will help. Maybe God will help. And that God will help. [INAUDIBLE] and this come. [INAUDIBLE] coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my street, we, we used to catch the train to go to the city to go to work, and I used to wait for the train. I saw an old man, a Yiddish man from 60, or 65, or 70 years of age. He was standing. And at one place he was standing was two big, big buildings, three floors tall building. And between the building was not close to the other. There was an empty space by the [INAUDIBLE]. He was standing there. A few boards was like a, like a bench. A little tin of the-- on the roof to, to to, to stop the rain coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was standing there, he was working. Warm, cold, hot, rain, stop, he was standing there work. I admired this man. He, he was-- what he-- work he doing? If you got a-- a-- a kettle, the kettle-- the handle from kettle get broken. You use to take to this man, he fix it. If you got a hole in the, in the pot, he-- he-- the man, he fix it. A Primus [NON-ENGLISH] but you know, a Primus? He fix it. I admired this man [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We, in our hostel-- I call it hostel-- was a big hall-- public hall, and he was-- this hall we were-- we was living, about 60, 50, 60 people, young people like we was in this same hall was living. And some people of this hall have Primus, to cook something. I have-- we have our Primus, too. One day, the Primus got broken, not working. I tell myself, no worry. I'll just take the Primus to this man and he'll fix it. I took the-- the Primus to this man.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tell him we are [NON-ENGLISH]. That means we escaped from German, from Hitler. We're living here in this hostel. Please, can you fix it, the Primus for tomorrow?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. Can't fix it for tomorrow, because tomorrow's [NON-ENGLISH], Fasting Day. Yiddish or religious people fasting. Not-- I will fast tomorrow. I will not working tomorrow. I can't say, you must do it, because you can't, if a man say like that. But come in my mind, if the man told me he's a-- according-- he's a religious man, something like that, this man will be able to help me with the chuppah I went-- I went home. I didn't ask him on the spot, because I didn't want it on the hot spot to-- to-- to the interview or something like that. I went home, I tell my Leja. She tells me, oh, that's [INAUDIBLE]. Next week or two weeks later, I was working the fifth-- the fifth shift, because I was working two shifts, the morning shift and the afternoon shift. I went into this man's-- I work, I worked in the fifth shift. I went into this man's work room. The man standing, he's doing his work. I tell him, I want to have a talk with you about a very important matter. He didn't took notice what I'm saying. He just say, can you tell me-- He just looks on me-- He just looked on me, he just-- and he still keeps, keep working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What important matter you have to talk with me? About a chuppah. You know what a chuppah mean? As I told him, chuppah, that means something to do with religion. And he's-- and he just fright. Maybe something can happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: He put away this screwdriver or the hammer, what he hold in the hand, and he turned and he said to me-- and he started scratching with his finger on his beard, started scratching, and he yell it out, I wanted to know with who I'm talking! I tell him quietly, you talk with a young man which escaped from Poland with his girlfriend. We was got-- we got married in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] office. But my girlfriend-- my wife, for she already a wife, she want to get married [NON-ENGLISH]. I told him three word in the Hebrew, in Hebrew. He saw I am speaking Hebrew, that's mean I'm not something like that. I see, hear what I'm speaking Hebrew [NON-ENGLISH] according to Jewish law.

speaker: interviewer
text: He turned his head to me, and I tell him, the reason why I'm coming to you, ask about chuppah, you remember I brought you a Primus to fix it? You told me, you can't fix it because it's [NON-ENGLISH], you're fasting. As he see what I'm talking about, he find out that it's not a monkey business. This is true. He told me, can you tell me your name, and your girlfriend's name, and your parents' name, but told me your Yiddish name, and the name what you call up to the Torah? Not [NON-ENGLISH] name like John, Jim, something like that. Yiddish name. I tell him my name is Mojsze. My girlfriend's name is Leja. Like that. Did you ever learned Torah? Jewish religion? I said, yes. Till 16 years of age, I learned. He said, I want to ask you some Torah questions. You will answer me? That's what you did, did, did. That's what, what the question.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, it depends what questions you will ask me. He asked me four or five questions, Torah questions. I answered the questions. As he saw that I am not-- that I know nothing, I know something, he pushed his hand to me, he said, mazel tov. He told me, Mojsze, when you go home to your hostel, in the corner, you will see a, a, a few cottages. One cottage, the front door is painted-- he tell me a color, red or green, I can't remember what color that he told me. You going into this cottage. You tell-- Zalman, my name is Zalman. You tell Zalman asked you to go there, and she, the lady will fix Leja's chuppah. When I went home, I didn't go in because I'm [INAUDIBLE] with Leja. I want to speak with Leja first, like it's to-- to discuss. And the question was, when I have off weekend, she didn't have, because we didn't work in the same place. If I have off Monday, she has Tuesday. If she has Tuesday, I got Wednesday, something like that. But between two-- a few weeks, comes together, we have one day off. When we have, bought of us, one-- the same day off, and we walk pass this place, I said to Leja, let's go in and tell him, and tell him about the chuppah. As he opened the door, I see about 15 or 50 Yiddish people sitting and singing songs. And the-- on the table was still a few bottles of, of soft drinks, a few pieces of bread. That's was-- according to Jewish religion, that's the third meal what Yiddish people have for Saturday. The first meal, they got on Friday night, second meal they got on Saturday, and the third meal, they got on Saturday between day and night. Not a special meal, just they dance, sing songs, like that.

words.text: I want to speak with Leja first,

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. As the man, Zalman-- You know, Zalman, the man, which-- which he repairman, he saw me, he stood up. He come to me. He grab me. Mojsze, forgive me my sins. What's that he done? But Zalman, what sin you done to me? I forgive you, but-- but tell me what. I sinned. I did a sin. What sin you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was coming my little workroom, he asked about a chuppah. I should shut the door and go, we fix it. I didn't do like that. I [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] I said, but Zalman, you're not ready, I'm not ready, and the people from the table wasn't. Zalman, you see Mojsze is an honest-- honest man. I tell him, in a few weeks' time, we will have-- I, with my wife, will have the same day off. We'll make the chuppah at that time. And I went, save it. All right. And that day, when we talk about the chuppah we come there to have, a big table, with a white tablecloth cover, everything on the table what prepared for our [INAUDIBLE] something like that. Bread, [INAUDIBLE], fish, meat, vodka, everything, everything what you needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tell Leja where do I get it from? At that time of the war, Russia in Finland. Bread was not enough to eat, but hard to get. And then they prepared everything, but I-- I couldn't understand where they get it. Leja tell me, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time was two men in-- in-- in-- in the house. One man start to write [NON-ENGLISH] the [INAUDIBLE]. He hasn't got any ink. He can't write. It not good to write with a pencil. They have to write with ink. He have not ink. You can see on the, on the original chuppah, the first line is, it's-- it's just-- just, can't see. The other man said, I got ink home. He went home, he brought some ink, we make the, the [INAUDIBLE]. And in-- in one hour, one hour or so, the house was full of people, about 60, 70 people. Man and wife were coming together, celebrating our-- our marriage. As I asked the-- I start to walk for one-- one-- one end room to the chuppah, I-- I tell to Leja, look, we've married in a strange land, a strange country, strange people. We don't know the people at all. And look what they are preparing. They're making us our-- our marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's our parents? Where's our mother? Where's our father? Where's our brothers? And when I was talking, when I was going from this end to this end, to the chuppah, I was ticking, but I was come across the chuppah, I stop talking, because I didn't want the-- the people to hear. We got a chuppah. We got a meal. I say the prayers, what I needed to say. After I finish-- after they finish, I ask Zalman, how much I got to pay you? Nothing! What? Look here, what you prepare, it cost money. Where you get? Nothing. He said nothing, and I say, I want to pay you. But the other man, next, said, well, Zalman, what is he? That man is a honest man. He does it for nothing. Let him get something. Give a donation. I give him some money. I can't remember how much how I give to him. And there, start another story. There. What you like in this story? Did you ever hear with any people what interview say something like that? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's not finished. That's not finished. I got plenty to say. Oh, dear, dear, dear.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after that. Please, let me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so you continued working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. I will tell you later on. You agree or not agree? Because it's a-- -- a-- a story that a very important story. Anyway, I will tell you.

words.text: I will tell you later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, after the marriage, the German Army occupied, start occupied Russia. They already moving so fast, they already near Vitebsk. And I think I, I'm sick with Leja what we should do? Shall we go, or we stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I said, all right, we'll either escape-- once is enough-- but I told myself-- I said, myself, no, I wouldn't stay by the Germans. I don't care about this big basket what I got, all the goods prepared for them. I don't care about them. I took out from the basket the pair of trousers. I get a shirt, this, that. My-- Leja took out something like that. We went the station. Went to the station and I called the lady, but she was looking after-- the, the, the, the cleaning lady, and I tell her, here's the key from this basket. You got full of goods. We are Jewish people. I don't want it's left-- stay here to be the Germans that come and kill me. The war will not last for long, I sure. After the war, we'll come back, you give me back the key, and I will pay you for you looking for after the basket. She said, oh, I will not touch it. I will not touch it. I will not do nothing. All right. They left. We went, went to the train. We catch the train. Next day, we was coming in a big town. The town name is Smolensk, Smolensk. This town is a very important town for Russia, and what a important town for Germany as well. Because from this town, from this railway station, you can go any part of Russia you wanted, direct. You don't have to change the train. Direct, Smolensk. As I say, the Germans was also interested in this town. They was coming and bombed our train. Six carriages destroyed completely. Many people-- every, the, every carriage was full of people-- destroyed. And we run out from the train to catch another train. Doesn't matter where the train is going, till we go away from the front line. After two days on the train, we find out the train is going to Uzbekistan. That is near Iran, near [INAUDIBLE], near Iran. We decided we not going out from the train till the train will come the last stop. Of course, we don't want it anymore. We come the last stop, a little town, [INAUDIBLE]. We was there, we start to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I'm finished. Go on. Go ahead. Go on. No. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I'm not, I'm not saying anymore. Finish. I told already. Ask-- ask question.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you came to that town, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We start to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we, we, we do it, any work you do in, in the [NON-ENGLISH]. It's near the [NON-ENGLISH], like a, a government place. We have a horse. Looking for the horse. And they used to make in-- in--  in-- infections. They used to spray it, spray it, infections, you know, a-- against the sickness. I was working. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you working there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long? Till I was call up in the army. Few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- And what did your wife do? Was she working?

speaker: interviewee
text: She--, she--, she--, she-- stopped there and she was working. She stopped there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what she was working. She was doing work. She was making dresses, and this and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you call-- what kind of accommodation did you have to live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- in this village?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shocking place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our house is built not of bricks, not of wood. Just some pieces of-- of-- of-- of-- of dirt. Make into like bricks, and they-- but that, that's what build the house. I remember one day I was sleeping, and the rain was coming on, and we took a-- we got a-- a big piece of plaster we put on the, on the ceiling. The rain not come on on the bed. And there was a-- that was so much water come on out on-- on this plaster, a big, big piece, a big piece, it fall on us. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then I was called up in the Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year you were called up in the Red Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43. In January, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what-- where-- Did your wife stay in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Stayed there, stopped there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what kind of unit were you called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- what unit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: For two weeks, I got training. What training? Just to learn how to shoot with a gun, and how to shoot with a machine gun. Two weeks. We didn't have anything else, just to know the-- because in the army, you learn this, you do that. Nothing. Just how to shoot with the machine gun, and how to shoot with the gun, that's all. And I was-- I was put up to the first White Russian front, under the command, Marshal Rokossovskii.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- Can you remember how to spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sure. R-O-K-O-W-S-K-I-E, Rokossovskii.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Verify his name, that I was, Marshal Rokossovskii.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were under his command?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not his. Not-- He was the command from--

speaker: interviewee
text: --all the [INAUDIBLE], from all-- all. But in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in the Army was maybe about 20, or 10, or 15 divisions. I don't know, but I got-- I don't know anything, but something like that. We was only in the regiment, and each regiment was 50 people, 60 people, something like that, fought in a regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we continue? We'll finish Tape Three here, and we'll continue onto Tape Four a moment. Thank you.
